title: The Summit
desc: A muge gathering. You understand about a third of what they're arguing about.

steps.text:
The Grand Hall of Muge's Summit is carved from a single chamber of crystalline stone that amplifies Mugic naturally. When the muges gather, the air itself hums with latent energy. When they argue, the walls shake.

They are currently arguing.

You've been assigned as an attendant — fetch water, carry documents, stay out of the way. The summit's agenda is printed on a scroll that uses words you recognize in arrangements that make no sense: "Resonance Frequency Calibration of the Tertiary Hive-Chord" and "Proposed Amendment to the Spore Propagation Standard."

Thirteen muges sit in a circle. One is shouting about harmonic intervals. Another is demonstrating a counter-argument by humming a note that makes your fillings ache. A third has fallen asleep.

"Water for station three," someone signals. You deliver water. The sleeping muge continues sleeping.

options.label: Focus on the resonance argument — it seems important
Focus on the spore debate — it sounds practical

steps.text:
The resonance argument is about the Hive-Chord — the mass Mugic that structurally reinforces Mount Pillar's foundations. Apparently, the third harmonic is slightly off-frequency, and the muges can't agree on why.

"It's the population shift," argues a tall muge with amber-stained hands. "Section fifteen was relocated. Their voices are no longer positioned correctly in the harmonic field."

"Irrelevant," counters a smaller muge, practically vibrating with disagreement. "The frequency is determined by the stone, not the singers. The stone has changed. Something in the deep rock has shifted the resonant frequency of the foundation."

This gets attention. Even the sleeping muge opens one eye.

"Shifted how?" someone asks.

"I don't know. But the tertiary frequency has dropped by point-three hertz since last quarter. That's not population variation — that's geological."

The room goes quiet. A point-three hertz shift might sound trivial, but in a system where thousands of voices must synchronize to reinforce stone, it's a significant drift. If the muges can't recalibrate the Hive-Chord to match the new frequency, the Mugic becomes noise instead of structure.

The summit assigns a research team: measure the foundation stone's natural frequency, determine what changed, recalibrate the chord. You deliver more water and pretend you understood half of it.

But you remember the number: point-three hertz. Something in the deep rock is different. And nobody knows why.

options.label: Leave the summit with a number you can't forget

steps.text:
The spore debate is more accessible. The Danian fungal gardens propagate by releasing spores into the tunnel air system — a controlled process that ensures the gardens spread uniformly. The current standard dictates spore release timing, density, and tunnel routing.

The problem: the spores are going where they shouldn't. Three non-garden tunnels have developed fungal growth in the past month, including a residential corridor where the bioluminescent fungus is now growing on people's walls.

"The residents don't mind," one muge argues. "Free lighting."

"The residents' walls are being structurally compromised by mycelial infiltration," another counters. "The mucus seal isn't rated for fungal growth. In six months, the wall coating will fail."

The debate spirals into a technical argument about spore aerodynamics, mucus chemistry, and whether the ventilation system's recent modifications created new airflow patterns that carry spores to unintended locations.

You carry documents between arguing factions. One proposal suggests modifying the ventilation. Another suggests changing the spore release schedule. A third, more radical proposal suggests genetically modifying the fungus to be less aggressive.

The summit compromises: adjust ventilation flow in the affected corridors and increase mucus seal thickness by ten percent in residential areas. The genetic modification proposal is tabled for "further study," which seems to mean "we're not ready to argue about that yet."

You leave the summit understanding more about fungal spore dynamics than you ever expected.
